content.text: The Rules of Hooks

React enforces exactly two rules for calling hooks. They are not arbitrary style guidelines — they are required for React to correctly associate hook state with the component that owns it across every render. Violating either rule produces bugs that range from subtle state corruption to a hard runtime crash with a clear error message.

attrs.alt: Valid vs invalid hook call sites for both rules

content.text: Why These Rules Exist: Hook Identity by Position
Every hook call is identified by its
position in the call order
, not by a name or key. React keeps a linked list of hook slots on each fiber node. The first
useState

call in a component maps to slot 0, the second to slot 1, the third to slot 2, and so on. On every subsequent render, React walks the same list in the same order, restoring each slot's state. If the order changes — because a hook was inside a condition that evaluated differently — the slots shift and every hook after the shift reads the wrong state.
Internally, each fiber node carries a
memoizedState

property that points to the head of a linked list of hook objects. Each hook object stores its current value and a pointer to the next hook object. React uses a "current hook" cursor that advances one position on each hook call during render. This design is why hooks work without any identifier — the cursor position
is
the identifier.
React identifies each hook by its position in the call order, not by a name. Changing the order changes which state each hook reads.
Rule 1: Only Call Hooks at the Top Level
Rule 1:
Only call hooks at the top level of a React function.
Do not call hooks inside loops, conditions, or nested functions.
Why Conditions Break Hook Order

Placing a hook inside a conditional gates whether that hook runs. When the condition is true on render N but false on render N+1, the hook at that position is skipped, shifting every subsequent hook's slot assignment by one. React detects this as a hook count mismatch and throws:
Rendered more hooks than during the previous render
or the inverse.
Why Loops Break Hook Order

Calling hooks inside a loop is equally broken. If the list has 3 items on render N, React allocates 3 slots for those loop hooks. If the list grows to 4, a fourth slot appears mid-list that didn't exist before, again shifting everything after it. The fix is to extract the loop body into a child component and call the hook at the top level of that child.
Nested Functions

Calling a hook inside an event handler or a helper function defined inside the component is a subtler violation. The function only runs on specific events, not on every render, so React never sees the hook call during render. Move the hook call to the top level of the component and pass the value down to the helper.
Applying Conditions After the Hook Call
When you want conditional behavior, call the hook unconditionally and apply the condition inside the hook's callback or after reading the value:
Rule 2: Only Call Hooks from React Functions
Rule 2:
Only call hooks from React function components or custom hooks.
Do not call hooks from regular JavaScript functions, class components, or event listeners.

Hooks rely on React's internal renderer being active when they are called. Outside a component render, there is no fiber context, no current hook cursor, and no way for React to associate the hook call with any component. Calling
useState
from a plain utility function or a class method throws:
Invalid hook call. Hooks can only be called inside of the body of a function component.
Custom Hooks as the Sanctioned Pattern
Custom hooks are the sanctioned escape hatch. A function whose name starts with
use
is treated by both React and the eslint plugin as a hook context. It can call built-in hooks, return any value, and be called from other hooks or from function components. The
use
prefix is a signal to tooling, not enforced by the runtime — but the linter will warn if you call a function starting with
use
from outside a hook or component.
Enforcing the Rules with ESLint
The
eslint-plugin-react-hooks
package ships two rules:
rules-of-hooks
enforces the call-site constraints at the editor level, and
exhaustive-deps
catches missing dependency array entries. Both ship at
"error"
severity in the recommended config. Install once, configure once, and let the linter catch violations before they reach runtime:
npm install --save-dev eslint-plugin-react-hooks
The plugin understands the
use
naming convention, so it also checks custom hooks. It reports violations with the specific line number, making the error easy to find even in large components.
Common Violations and Fixes
Conditional State Initialization
The most common violation is conditionally fetching data: developers write
if (!data) { const [state, setState] = useState(null); }
trying to defer initialization. The fix is to always call
useState
unconditionally and initialize with
null
or a suitable default:
Helper Functions That Should Be Custom Hooks
The second most common violation is abstracting hook calls into a helper function that is not a custom hook. If
setupSubscription()
calls
useEffect
inside it, and it starts with a lowercase letter that is not
use
, the linter won't flag it as a hook, and the call might come from a class method or utility. Rename to
useSubscription
and call it only from a component or another hook.
Hooks in Array.map Callbacks
Hooks inside
Array.prototype.map
callbacks are another frequent mistake. Mapping over a list of items and calling
useState
per item looks appealing but violates rule 1. Each component in the rendered list should manage its own state — move the hook call into the child component, not the mapper.
Diagnosing the "Rendered More Hooks" Error

If you receive the "rendered more hooks" error, check three things in order: whether any hook is behind a conditional, whether any hook is inside a loop or array method, and whether the component is being rendered by two different React instances (common when mixing a locally installed React with a library's bundled React).
Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
{'type': 'text', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}], 'text': 'Rendered more hooks than during the previous render'}
Hook inside condition or loop
Move hook to top level; put condition inside effect or after hook call
{'type': 'text', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}], 'text': 'Rendered fewer hooks than during the previous render'}
Early return before a hook call
Move all hook calls before any early return
{'type': 'text', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}], 'text': 'Invalid hook call'}
Hook called outside a function component or custom hook
Move to component body or extract into a custom hook
Two React instances detected
Library bundled its own React alongside your app's React
Ensure single React copy via package deduplication
Strict Mode and Double Invocation

React's strict mode (in development) double-invokes the function body of every component on mount to surface side effects that depend on run count. This does not change hook behavior but can expose hidden rule violations — if state is unexpectedly reset after the second invocation, a hook is being called somewhere it should not be.
React DevTools and Hook State

React DevTools shows hook state in two places. The Components panel displays each hook's current value next to its type (State, Effect, Ref, etc.) in the order they are called. If a hook order changes between renders, DevTools will show a flash of incorrect state before the error is thrown. This makes DevTools useful for visually confirming that hook order is stable.
Custom hooks appear in DevTools with their function name prefixed by the
use

keyword. Hooks called inside the custom hook are shown as children, giving you a tree view of composed hook state. You can also pin hook values to watch them change across renders without using console.log.
The use() Hook Exception in React 19
React 19 introduced
use()
, a new hook that can read a promise or context value. Unlike other hooks,
use()
is exempt from Rule 1 in one specific way: it
can

be called inside a conditional. This is intentional — it lets you avoid reading a resource when a condition means the data is not needed. It remains subject to Rule 2 (only call from React functions).
The exemption is narrow: only
use()
has it, because React Compiler can statically analyze its placement. All other hooks remain bound by both rules. Do not infer from
use()
that conditional hooks are acceptable in general — the linter continues to flag any other hook inside a conditional.
Rules Quick-Reference Checklist
A quick mental checklist before committing any hook call:
Is the hook at the very top of the function body?

content: If not, move it above any conditions, loops, or nested function definitions.

content.text: Is the calling function a React component or a custom hook?

content: If it's a class method, a utility function, or an event listener, move the hook or wrap in a custom hook.

content.text: Is the hook inside a .map(), .filter(), or .forEach() callback?

content: Extract the callback into a child component.

content.text: Is the hook inside an if/else block?

content: Move the hook above the conditional and apply the condition inside the hook's effect or after reading its value.

content.text: Does the function name start with 'use'?

content: If the function calls hooks but doesn't start with 'use', rename it so the linter can validate it.

content.text: Summary

The rules of hooks are not arbitrary constraints — they are a direct consequence of how React identifies hook state: by call order within a render, not by name. Calling hooks at the top level unconditionally preserves a stable call order across every render. Calling hooks only from React functions ensures the renderer's internal cursor is active. The eslint plugin enforces both rules automatically, so in practice violations are caught before they reach runtime.
Rule 1 — top level only

content: : hooks identify state by call-order position; any branch or loop that changes which hooks run corrupts the slot mapping

content.text: Rule 2 — React functions only

content: : hooks require an active fiber context; plain JS functions, class methods, and event listeners have none

content.text: ESLint enforcement
eslint-plugin-react-hooks
"error"
Conditions belong inside hooks

content: : call the hook unconditionally, apply the condition inside the effect callback or after reading the value

content.text: Loops belong in child components

content: : each component manages its own hook state — move the hook into the child rather than the mapper

content.text: Custom hooks are the reuse pattern
useX

content: can call any hook and can be called from any component or other hook
